one_line_takeaway: The paper derives, from first-principles hypothesis testing, the information-theoretically optimal coupling between watermark side-information and token-vocabulary partitioning for single-token LLM watermarking, yielding the distortion-free 'Correlated Channel' scheme that empirically beats the red-green watermark on perplexity and generation quality at matching or better detection Z-scores on WaterBench.
key_finding: On WaterBench (Table 1), CC attains the lowest perplexity in every one of the six tasks, improving PPL over red-green by up to 45% and gaining up to +6.6 GM points on Knowledge Understanding, while CC with k=3 matches or exceeds red-green's Z-scores at δ=1 across all tasks (peaking at Z=4.2 on Longform QA), and CC with k=2 still yields statistically significant Z-scores (approximately 0.6-3.4) at essentially zero perceptual/fidelity cost, since CC is constructed to be exactly distortion-free (zero-perception, Rp=0.5) by design. On synthetic uniform token distributions (m=10, |S|=2), CC with balanced-partition sampling attains the theoretical-optimum detection probability of 0.75 at zero perception, versus 0.70 for the simpler i.i.d. Bernoulli sampling -- a gain of about 0.07 -- and matches the GUROBI-computed numerical optimum; red-green only reaches the same detection level as CC in the limit δ→∞ at the cost of nonzero perception, crossing the suboptimal Bernoulli-CC curve near δ≈7.6. Increasing the side-information alphabet size k increases detection with diminishing returns, and performance degrades when m is not divisible by k because balanced partitions cannot be formed.
core_contribution: The paper formulates single-token ('one-shot') LLM watermarking as a hypothesis-testing problem with side information and derives the fundamental trade-off between watermark detection power (Rd) and textual-distortion/perception (Rp). It proves the optimal detection probability is achieved by a coupling between the shared side information S and a randomized partition of the vocabulary generated via a maximum-coupling construction, which the authors term the Correlated Channel (CC) watermark. They give closed-form expressions for the minimax detection rate under a worst-case, min-entropy-constrained next-token distribution (Theorems 1-4), show the optimal partition-randomization strategy is sampling uniformly over balanced (equal-count) token partitions, and prove a near-optimal i.i.d. Bernoulli sampling approximation whose error decays as O(1/m) in vocabulary size m. They extend the one-shot analysis heuristically to sequential (multi-token) watermarking and empirically validate CC against the red-green (Kirchenbauer et al.) watermark on synthetic distributions and on real LLM generation using the WaterBench benchmark.
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model in the security-exploit sense. The paper frames three parties: Alice (the LLM/watermark generator), Bob (the detector, who holds the shared side-information key S), and Charlie (an outside observer without access to S who tries to judge whether text looks watermarked/distorted, i.e. a proxy for 'imperceptibility' rather than an active adversary). The 'worst case' analyzed is an adversarial choice of the LLM's next-token distribution QX (bounded by a min-entropy/inf-norm constraint) for which the CC scheme's detection guarantee must still hold -- this is a minimax robustness analysis over token-distribution shapes, not a study of evasion attacks, paraphrasing attacks, or key-extraction attacks against deployed watermarks. No adversarial removal/spoofing attacks are modeled or evaluated in this paper.
limitations: The paper's own stated limitations and scope restrictions: (1) the core theoretical results (Theorems 1-4) apply strictly to the one-shot (single-token, n=1) watermarking setting; the extension to sequential/multi-token watermarking (Section 4, Proposition 4) is only rigorously analyzed under the simplifying i.i.d.-token assumption, and the authors explicitly state they 'leave the theoretical analysis of the token-level extension of our scheme to future work' for the general autoregressive case. (2) The minimax optimality result assumes a min-entropy (inf-norm) constrained adversarial QX, not the actual empirical distribution of real LLM next-token probabilities, so the closed-form guarantees do not directly transfer to the LLM experiments in Section 6. (3) The overall optimization problem (Eq. 4) is non-convex and generally lacks a closed-form solution outside the corner points characterized in the paper. (4) The paper does not evaluate robustness to adversarial watermark removal, paraphrasing, or spoofing/forgery attacks -- it is a detection-power/text-quality trade-off analysis, not an adversarial robustness study. (5) LLM experiments are run on a single benchmark (WaterBench) with two settings each of CC (k=2,3) and red-green (δ=1,3); no larger-scale or production-model evaluation is reported.

datasets_used: WaterBench benchmark (six tasks: Knowledge Understanding, Knowledge Memorization, Finance QA, Longform QA, Multi-news, LCC)
synthetic uniform and two-point token distributions (m=10, m=20, m=60 vocabulary sizes)
quantitative_results: Synthetic m=10, |S|=2, uniform QX, zero perception: CC balanced-partition detection Rd=0.75 (matches theoretical optimum/GUROBI solver); CC i.i.d. Bernoulli sampling Rd=0.70
Red-green watermark reaches CC's detection level only in the limit delta->infinity; crosses the Bernoulli-CC curve at delta ~= 7.6

WaterBench Knowledge Understanding (baseline GM=32.76): CC k=2 PPL=0.1594, GM=31, Z=0.6448; CC k=3 PPL=0.1047, GM=33.2, Z=0.051; RG d=1 PPL=0.2098, GM=30, Z=1.37974; RG d=3 PPL=0.14977, GM=26.58, Z=1.50712

WaterBench Longform QA (baseline GM=21.88): CC k=2 PPL=0.2346, GM=21.17, Z=3.393; CC k=3 PPL=0.19381, GM=21.76, Z=4.1967 (paper's cited peak Z=4.2); RG d=1 PPL=0.2883, GM=21.32, Z=2.5836; RG d=3 PPL=0.3063, GM=21.04, Z=5.86793

Overall: CC attains lowest PPL in all six WaterBench tasks; improves PPL over red-green by up to 45% and GM by up to +6.6 points (Knowledge Understanding); CC k=2 Z-scores range approx 0.6-3.4 at near-zero perception cost
Theorem 3 closed-form minimax detection rate R*_d(lambda) for k=2, m large: penalty term equals lambda/4 for lambda in [0.5,1], with cost of worst-case QX lower-bounded by 1/8
For k=2, detection probability upper-bounded by Rd <= 3/4 (Proposition 3), achieved when the partition variable Y ~ Bernoulli(1/2)
baselines_compared: Red-green (RG) watermark of Kirchenbauer et al. 2023 (also called Green/Red list watermark) at tilting parameters delta=1 and delta=3 (and a delta sweep in ROC plots)
exact GUROBI-based numerical solver of the non-convex detection-perception optimization (Eq. 4)
